item.title: Wow! What a woman! She also trains others to be able to do it!
item.description: Here's her website for anyone who might be interested! https://www.basmahameed.com/ Her training academy https://www.basmahameedtrainingacademy.com/
item.dc:creator: DumpingSh1tandBeingKind2

item.title: Ever year
item.description: I try to post ths every year, as a reminder that even when our nation shines brightest, we are still capable of terrifying things. The illegal interment of American citizens based solely on their race was not the worse thing the US did during world war 2. And it is far from the worst thing that we have done in our history. We should be proud of our accomplishments, defeating the NAZIs and Nationalist in Japan is a great and worthy deed. Insisting that the European powers dismantle their colonial empire post war is a frequently forgotten accomplishment. But we must also be humble and remember the lesson that we are capable of terrible things. Some will try to justify terribleness as necessary, as a mercy to bring wars to swift conclusion. But a simple review will show that the interment of American citizens and the firebombings of civilians did nothing to bring the war to a close. Those things took resources away from the war effort. Remember that democracy is a constant struggle, one that we put ourselves in because we claim the responsibility of governing ourselves. VOTE.
item.dc:creator: sevenfingerman

item.title: As Monday's go this was the shittiest!
item.description: Wasn't going to post about it but there is something to be said about screaming into the void! I really am having a difficult time dealing with all of it. This was not something I expected to make most viral. I just needed to get shit off my chest.

item.title: Nanna's 18th birthday
item.description:
Hi everyone, I'm MrPuckett Today is my dog's 18th birthday!!!
If you know the story of Nanna you know this is the most unexpected of birthdays We were all pretty sure she would die within a couple weeks of coming home
This was Nanna in the shelter two and a half years ago Her previous owners surrendered her at 15 years old blind, deaf, terminal heart disease, incontinence, skin/ear/eye infections and arthritis so bad she could barely stand up.
So I took her home. I figured I could show her a fun couple weeks or months before she dies I prepared every meal for her from scratch, we did canine stretches every day and I just wanted her to be comfortable for once She was in so much pain.
I pushed her all over Nashville in a stroller and slowly started to see her having fun She walked 11 steps on our first adventure and every day we went a little bit further
The healthier Nanna got the clearer it became how neglected she was her entire life She wasn't close to death because she was old, she was close to death because she never got the care she desperately needed.
The progress she made in the first year was amazing She walked around the block for the first time, then she did her first mile a few months later, at 16 years old she did her first 5k And today, on her 18th birthday -
Nanna crushed her first 10k ever ＼⁠(⁠°⁠o⁠°⁠)⁠／ The 5k at 16 years old took her 108 minutes The 10k today took her 107 minutes, it's unbelievable progress!
With all the progress it's important for me not to forget the bigger reality Nanna is 18 years old, full of comorbidities and she's going to die a hell of a lot sooner than later.
That's why it's so important for me to celebrate all of these milestones. I've really made it a point this year to take her to as many new places as possible, feed her as many new things as I can and just try to experience as much as possible before it's too late
I've never really had a dog that needed to be taken care of before, my other dogs are so independent but Nanna needs care just about every waking moment. It's brought some of my toughest days in addiction recovery, and when things go right like they did today, some of the most beautiful moments as well.
This is my 18 year old puppy Nanna Pants She deserves to have the happiest birthday in history.
With her birthday every year we kick off Puckett Week - supporting domestic violence survivors facing homelessness
The supplies Imgur sent to abuse survivors in the first year of Puckett Week couldn't even fit in the picture frame it was amazing You might be wondering why domestic violence survivors? What does that have to do with me or dogs?
I was homeless for years with a small incredible dog named Mr Puckett It was the most terrifying time of our lives.
As domestic violence contributes to homelessness for women and children more than any other cause, supporting these families is a way to keep them off the street entirely The Mary Parrish Center has beautiful apartment homes where survivors can stay safe and start their life fresh with all the support they need
The Mary Parrish Center set up an Amazon Wishlist where you can send household items to abuse survivors staying in their safe apartments When residents are back on their feet and ready to move out, they even get to take everything in the furnished apartments to make the transition easier
Or if you'd like to donate $18 in honor of Nanna's 18th birthday I'll include the link to Puckett Week on their website below. It's a cause and an organization that means a lot to me and I'm always hopeful it means a lot to everyone else as well.
I don't know if this will be Nanna's last birthday ever, but I'm sure as hell going to treat her as if it is Every food she wants, every adventure she wants...
Anything to pay her back for all the love and joy she's brought into my life Happy birthday Nanna Pants, we all love you so goddamn much. ... To donate $18 to abuse survivors in honor of Nanna's 18th birthday - https://www.maryparrish.org/donate ... If you'd like to send supplies to the survivors of domestic violence at Mary Parrish - https://www.amazon.com/hz/wishlist/ls/3USHUHIVSINQE?ref_=wl_share ... Thank you so much for reading my post, more on the other Pucketts later this week!
item.dc:creator: MrPuckett

item.title: Weird Al conducting the Jr. Philharmonic Orchestra of California at the 54th annual "Battle of Batons" (May 29, 1991).
item.description:
Looney Tunes at it's best
According to a commenter: "The thing that these malding goobers dont realize is that he is giving each instrument cues for their respective parts, he is keeping the time consistently, and he is in fact conducting. No matter how silly it is, he is perfectly conducting the orchestra." https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ssa1EnJt9UI
item.dc:creator: Herebychoice

item.title: Time to Enjoy
item.description:
Mercury through cloth
You scared me!
Use your skills!
Enjoy, we're partying
Follow the team
Run as much as you can
The End

item.title: Cake for Imgur's (My Husband's) Bday
item.description:
I bought a mini bundt cake to celebrate my husband's birthday. Yup - I'm Imgur's wife... It's this whole ongoing saga: https://imgur.com/gallery/9NkiCni
I ate it in front of my laptop with Imgur open so they could watch... Is this post weird enough yet? I feel like Upvote Everything Day is the only time I get to be as weird as I want to be on here so I had to grab this opportunity :P

item.title: Car wash worker power washes lady who threw a cup of lemonade at her.
item.description: Anna Harycki, 18, became a viral sensation for spraying water from a powerful hose through the open window of a rude customer's vehicle while working at an Indiana car wash … Harycki, who is a student, said that her employer sided with her to ensure her workplace safety. "I told my managers, who were not OK with [the customer] throwing a drink at me," she said. "They decided to ban her and her boyfriend from the car wash."
item.dc:creator: WakeUp2Yourself

item.title: Shitty start, but with good eggs
item.description: Norway, so it won’t even cost my mom anything but a small fee of €50-100 max Edit: We’re back now. Doc said it was a sudden drop in blood pressure paired with low intake of fluids in the morning, which she usually takes a lot of. The drop + little fluids + drawing of a regular blood sample at the doctors office combined into loss of consciousness. 3 stitches in the split lip and a future check at the dentist office and she’s sent home. Since it was a head injury(light concussion) they had her assigned 24 hour supervision by yours truly. Truly appreciate all the considerate thoughts and well wishes guys, you’re all awesome <3
item.dc:creator: JarlmannViking

item.title: 15 Years Ago Terry Pratchett Was Knighted by the Queen
item.description: “I meant," said Ipslore bitterly, "what is there in this world that truly makes living worthwhile?" Death thought about it. CATS, he said eventually. CATS ARE NICE.” ― Terry Pratchett, Sourcery

item.title: Every Regeneration from "Doctor Who", in honor of Imgur's 15th Regen-err... Birthday
item.description:
Hope @SpockIsMyHomeboy doesn't mind my borrowing his gif What started as a joke post on Imgur's 11th cakeday turned into a tradition, since the number of Doctors coincided with Imgur birthdays. There won't be a 16th Doctor next year, so I decided to go out with a bang.
By 1966, it was increasingly clear that William Hartnell's health was deteriorating and he was becoming more difficult to work with. Producer John Wiles had, following several clashes with Hartnell, intended to have the actor replaced in The Celestial Toymaker; during two episodes of that serial, the Doctor is invisible (owing to Hartnell being on holiday during the recording). Wiles' plan was for the character to reappear played by a new actor. This proposal was vetoed by Gerald Savory, the BBC's Head of Serials (and Wiles' superior), which led to Wiles leaving before The Celestial Toymaker was produced. During production, Hartnell and producer Innes Lloyd had reached an agreement that he should leave the role, having starred in one more serial that would see a handover to a new actor, which would be the first one produced as part of Season 4. Script editor Gerry Davis proposed that, since the Doctor had already been established as an alien, the character could die and return in a new body. Lloyd took this further by suggesting that the Doctor could do this "renewal" regularly, transforming from an older man to a younger one; this would allow for the convenient recasting of the role when necessary. The process itself was modelled on LSD trips, with the experience being like the "hell and dank horror" of taking the drug. At the conclusion of The Tenth Planet, the First Doctor falls unconscious, having commented earlier to his companions Ben and Polly that his body was "wearing a bit thin". Then, his features shift into that of the Second Doctor, played by Patrick Troughton. In The Power of the Daleks, the Second Doctor's first story, the Doctor draws an analogy between the renewal and a caterpillar turning into a butterfly. Most of the footage from Troughton's first episode is lost, like most early episodes of "Doctor Who".
When Troughton left the series in 1969, the Doctor was renewed again, but this time the change was forced on him by the Time Lords at the conclusion of The War Games, where it is referred to as a "change of appearance". As with the first change, this language suggested only a superficial physical change, not one of personality, although Jon Pertwee's portrayal of the Third Doctor also differed quite substantially from Troughton's. Unlike the previous change, this one is treated as a punishment rather than a natural process: in The War Games the Second Doctor protests, "You can't just change what I look like without consulting me!" The actual regeneration was also the only one never shown on screen, Pertwee simply opened his series having already undergone the regeneration. It was not because of footage being lost, it simply was never filmed. Likely due to Pertwee having yet been cast in the role. We only have fan-made videos like this one to rely on.
As the series continued, more aspects of the regenerative process were introduced, but the basic concepts of regeneration as accepted by fans of the series today were only firmly established in the final scene of Planet of the Spiders (1974), when Pertwee's Third Doctor turns into Tom Baker's Fourth Doctor. In this scene, the change is referred to with the word "regenerate" for the first time, and is explained as a biological process — "the cells will regenerate" — that occurs when a Time Lord's body is dying. It is also stated that following the regeneration the Doctor's brain cells would be shaken up and his behaviour would be "erratic" for a time, something that would be evident for most subsequent regenerations.
After succeeding Jon Pertwee's Third Doctor, Baker's portrayal of the Fourth was not initially received enthusiastically by all of the critics. One writer in the Daily Mail complained in early 1975 that, "Mr. Baker makes Doctor Who look like Harpo Marx let loose from Horse Feathers." Baker himself responded by saying, "We are not playing Doctor Who for laughs. I am trying to stress his strangeness, that he is not of this world, not human, therefore his reactions will be different from ours. I take it all very seriously. He has to be genuinely lovable, not pleased by violence, and he must be honest. Humorous, but never comical. His incarnation eventually became the most highly regarded among fans of the show, and considered one of the most iconic incarnations of the character. Baker became the most popular and recognisable of the Doctors, especially in the United States. Baker portrayed the character for seven consecutive seasons, which remains the longest tenure of any actor to portray the lead, counting both the classic and modern series. After Tom Baker had announced that he was leaving the role, the show's producers decided that the next Doctor was to be played by someone who presented something of a physical contrast to Baker and by an actor who was already firmly established in the British public's mind. Peter Davison was chosen due to his critically acclaimed role as Tristan Farnon in the BBC series "All Creatures Great and Small" which had Doctor Who producer John Nathan-Turner as line producer.
Baker's portrayal of the Sixth Doctor was not without controversy, with fans criticizing his bombastic and overbearing personality, as well as his penchant for using deadly force against enemies (to say nothing of his first episode where he tried to strangle Peri, his companion). Michael Grade had taken over as Controller of BBC 1 in 1984, and was not a fan of the series. In fact, he later admitted in interviews that he "hated" the programme, and he wanted to cancel it outright. There is much debate, however, as to how far his decisions were driven by his personal views. At the time, the BBC was suffering a financial shortfall due to expensive ventures such as the launch of EastEnders, breakfast and daytime television, and savings were needed across the corporation. "Doctor Who" was also up against the US import "The A-Team" in the Saturday Night ratings. In any case, when it was announced that Doctor Who's production would be moved back a financial year, the news was interpreted as that the show was under threat of cancellation. The press and public outcry was much larger than Grade or the Board of Governors of the BBC had expected, being given a full-page front cover story in the popular tabloid newspaper The Sun. Baker's era was interrupted by what would be a long 18-month hiatus between seasons 22 and 23, compared to the then-usual gap of nine months between seasons. Before its postponement, plans for season 23 were well advanced, with scripts already drafted and in at least one case distributed to cast and production workers. Having been off the air for eighteen months, the show found it hard to regain viewers who had turned to ITV. Saward and Nathan-Turner had decided on an overarching storyline for the entire season entitled "The Trial of a Time Lord", but its complexities proved confusing to both writers and viewers, with the season drawing viewing figures of only four to five million. Robert Holmes, who had returned to writing for the series on a semi-regular basis in 1984, died before he could deliver the final episode. In addition, Saward and Nathan-Turner had a falling out, with Saward resigning from the programme, and Nathan-Turner unofficially taking on the role of script editor after Saward's departure. Despite all of this, Grade consented to allow the series to continue, and moved it away from Saturday nights into a mid-week slot once more, and limited it again to a season of only fourteen 25-minute episodes. He also ordered that a new Doctor be found, as he was not enamoured of Colin Baker's portrayal. Baker was therefore dismissed from the role, despite Nathan-Turner's pleas to the BBC management that Baker did not fulfill his three-year contract, having effectively completed the equivalent of only one-and-a-half seasons.
The regeneration of the Sixth Doctor into the Seventh is the only time that a single actor took on the roles of two incarnations of the Doctor. Colin Baker declined the invitation to film the regeneration sequence at the start of Time and the Rani (1987) due to the circumstances in which the BBC dismissed him from the role. As a result, Sylvester McCoy had to don his predecessor's costume and a blond curly wig, lying face down, with the mixing effect to the Doctor's "new" features occurring as he was turned over. Although Michael Grade had left the BBC in 1987 to take up a new position as Chief Executive of Channel 4, Doctor Who was in a poor timeslot on Mondays opposite "Coronation Street" in seasons of only fourteen 25-minute episodes, and continued to suffer in the ratings. Jonathan Powell, the new Controller of BBC 1, regarded Nathan-Turner with contempt, and the BBC's new Head of Series, Peter Cregeen, decided to cancel the series, a decision which was clear to the production team by the end of production on the twenty-sixth season in August 1989. The final story to be produced as part of the original run was Ghost Light, although it was not the last to be broadcast. That was "Survival", the last episode of which was transmitted on 6 December 1989, and brought the series' twenty-six-year run to a close. John Nathan-Turner decided close to transmission that a more suitable conclusion should be given to the final episode as it was possibly going to be the last ever. Accordingly, Andrew Cartmel wrote a short, melancholic closing monologue for Sylvester McCoy, which McCoy recorded on 23 November 1989 – by coincidence, the show's twenty-sixth anniversary. This was dubbed over the closing scene as the Doctor and his companion Ace walked off into the distance, apparently to further adventures.
First time in modern America... and the Doctor gets shot & killed. Producer Philip Segal (a British ex-patriate) had been trying for some years to revive "Doctor Who" after its cancellation in 1989. His idea was to launch a new American-produced version of the series, like "Sanford & Son" and "All in the Family" were remakes of British shows. But the Fox Network — the only American network that showed any interest — was only prepared to commit to a single telemovie. It was hoped that, should the telemovie be successful, Fox might be persuaded to reconsider a series (with the movie as a backdoor pilot). However, the 1996 telemovie's ratings performance in America was not strong enough to hold Fox's interest. Sylvester McCoy appeared briefly at the beginning of the TV film, before "regenerating" into the Eighth Doctor as played by Paul McGann. McGann had been Segal's first choice for the part, although both the actor himself and the Fox Network had not initially been keen. Segal later claimed that the BBC's Executive Producer on the project, Jo Wright, had wanted the role of the "previous Doctor" to be played by Tom Baker, as it was felt he was regarded as the definitive Doctor by the British public and McCoy's tenure had not been as popular, but she backed down when Segal explained how this went against the continuity of the programme. Segal also had to fight to retain the familiar Doctor Who theme: composer John Debney wanted to write a new piece, but was convinced to create a rearrangement of Ron Grainer's composition, although Grainer did not receive screen credit for his work.
Despite the fact the Eighth Doctor appeared on television only three times, he is the most prolific of all the Doctors (to date) in terms of number of individual stories published in novel, novella, short story and audio form. One of those television appearances was in 2013, a minisode called "Night of the Doctor" that served as a prelude to the 50th anniversary special. This appearance marked the Eighth Doctor's final adventure and his regeneration into the "War Doctor" (a secret incarnation played by John Hurt). McGann reprised his role as the Eighth Doctor in the final Thirteenth Doctor special "The Power of the Doctor" (2022), as one of the “Guardians of the Edge” in an afterlife, inside the Doctor’s mind. He, along with the First (David Bradley), Fifth (Davison), Sixth (Colin Baker), and Seventh (McCoy) Doctors.
In his original conception of the show's anniversary special, Moffat had written the Ninth Doctor as having ended the Time War. However, he was "pretty certain" that Christopher Eccleston would decline to return to the role, which he did (due to clashing with BBC execs and Russell T. Davies at the end of his single series run). As he also had reservations about making Paul McGann's Eighth Doctor the incarnation who had ended the war, Moffat created a never-before-seen past incarnation of the Doctor, which allowed him "a freer hand" in writing the story, acknowledging that the success of doing this would be predicated on being able to cast an actor with a significant enough profile. At the end of the 50th anniversary special, once inside his TARDIS, he begins to regenerate. Realising that his body is "wearing a bit thin"... echoing the First Doctor's utterances in The Tenth Planet. This fanvid version shows more of Eccleston, whereas the original scene only hinted at his appearance.
Lorraine Heggessey, the first woman to be Controller of BBC One, proved to be equally enthusiastic about the idea of new Doctor Who, often commenting to the press that she would like to pursue the idea but that "rights difficulties" prevented it. Similarly positive comments were made by the corporation's overall Head of Drama, Jane Tranter. In September 2003, Heggessey managed to persuade Worldwide that as several years had now passed and they were no nearer to producing a film, BBC television should be allowed to make a new series. The other proposals notwithstanding, Tranter and Heggessey elected to approach Russell T. Davies once again, who had often told the BBC when approached for other projects that he would only return to them to take charge of a new series of Doctor Who. He quickly accepted, and on 26 September 2003 it was officially announced that Doctor Who would be returning to BBC One, produced in-house at BBC Wales in 2004 for transmission in 2005. Eccleston's reasons for leaving the role continued to be debated in Britain's newspapers: on 4 October 2005 Alan Davies told The Daily Telegraph that Eccleston had been "overworked" by the BBC, and had left the role because he was "exhausted". Eccleston later stated that he left the show because he "didn't enjoy the environment or the culture that the cast and crew had to work in", but that he was proud of having played the role. Eccleston said in a subsequent interview, "My relationship with my three immediate superiors – the showrunner, the producer and co-producer – broke down irreparably during the first block of filming and it never recovered." Despite his short tenure, Eccleston is still considered one of the most popular modern incarnations of the Doctor.
Tennant's time as the Tenth Doctor is highly regarded among fans of the show and is considered one of the most iconic incarnations of the character, often ranked alongside Tom Baker's Fourth Doctor. On 20 May 2008 it was announced that Russell T Davies would step down as executive producer and head writer of Doctor Who in 2009; Steven Moffat was confirmed as his replacement. On 29 October 2008, during his acceptance speech via live feed at the National Television Awards, David Tennant publicly announced his intention to exit the series at the end of the 2009 specials, making way for a new actor to portray the eleventh incarnation of the Doctor in the 2010 series. 26-year-old actor Matt Smith was announced as his successor in a special edition of Doctor Who Confidential, broadcast on BBC One on 3 January 2009.
Although Steven Moffat anticipated choosing a middle-aged actor for the new Doctor, Smith was aged 26 when cast, making him the youngest actor to portray the Doctor, three years younger than Peter Davison was at the time he began his role as the Fifth Doctor. Show producers were cautious about casting Smith because they felt that a 26-year-old could not play the Doctor adequately; BBC Wales Head of Drama Piers Wenger shared the sentiment, but noted that Smith was capable enough to play the role. Prior to auditioning for the role, Smith had never seen Doctor Who. He prepared for the role by writing his own stories featuring The Doctor, and took special inspiration from Patrick Troughton's portrayal of the Second Doctor.
Due partly to budget restrictions at the BBC, the expected 2012 series, Smith's third, was split into 5 episodes transmitted from September 2012 and a further 8 transmitted from April 2013, with the now traditional Christmas special between these 2 groups of episodes. Gillan and Darvill departed from the show at the end of the first group of episodes; new companion Clara Oswald debuted in the 2012 Christmas special, played by Jenna Coleman. Smith stepped down as the Eleventh Doctor with his last episode being the 2013 Christmas special. On 4 August 2013 in a live TV special, Peter Capaldi was revealed as the Twelfth Doctor. "It's been an honour to play this part," the actor announced in an official statement, "to follow the legacy of brilliant actors, and helm the TARDIS for a spell with 'the ginger, the nose and the impossible one'. But when ya gotta go, ya gotta go..." He had apparently agonized over the decision though, as he told the Los Angeles Times: "I'd have very happily done another year. It's a wonderful show. But I think you gotta go when you gotta go, and for me, it just felt like the right time to move on." Most tenures as the Doctor have lasted only three years.
During his appearance in the special, Capaldi said that he had been a fan of the series; a letter that he had written to the Radio Times at the age of 15 about the show was read aloud. Capaldi had already appeared in the series 4 episode "The Fires of Pompeii" as Caecilius, the father of the family that the Doctor saves from the eruption of Mount Vesuvius (later retconned as being the reason this incarnation chose this specific face). He had also appeared in "Torchwood: Children of Earth" as the civil servant John Frobisher... the less said about that character, the better. Capaldi kept his native Scottish accent for the role. Speaking on his decision, he said he did it so he could feel closer to the character.
Capaldi's run as the Doctor was plagued by long hiatuses between series and Christmas specials. Which didn't help the ratings at all. On 22 January 2016, the BBC announced that Moffat would be stepping down as showrunner after series 10 of Doctor Who and would be replaced by Chris Chibnall as lead writer and executive producer. In addition, series 10 would debut in Spring 2017, with a Christmas special airing in 2016.[42] On 30 January 2017, Peter Capaldi announced he would be stepping down as the Twelfth Doctor in the 2017 Christmas special.
Regarding his decision to leave, Capaldi stated that despite his tremendous love for the show, he was unsure if he would be able to deliver at his best performance if he remained in the role for too long. "This could be my final year; it's terrifying, I love Doctor Who but it can be an insular world and I do want to do other things. There will come a time when this is over, but I knew that when I started. I was thinking about my regeneration scene from the outset, that's my terrible melancholic nature, when you accept the job you know there'll come a day, inevitably, when you'll be saying goodbye."
On 14 July 2017, the BBC announced that the portrayer of the Thirteenth Doctor would be revealed after the 2017 Wimbledon Championships men's finals on 16 July 2017. JodieWhittaker was introduced as the Thirteenth Doctor on 16 July and subsequently made her debut in the 2017 Christmas special "Twice Upon a Time" and starring in the eleventh series. On Whittaker's casting, Chibnall said, "I always knew I wanted the Thirteenth Doctor to be a woman and we're thrilled to have secured our number one choice. Her audition for The Doctor simply blew us all away." Later on in his statement, Chibnall called Whittaker "an in-demand, funny, inspiring, super-smart force of nature" and said that she "will bring loads of wit, strength and warmth to the role."
Despite positive reception to Whittaker's portrayal, her run as the Doctor was marred by criticisms of Chris Chibnall's writing, which resulted in a serious decline in the ratings for the series. Faced with possible cancellation (like what happened in the late 80s with the 6th and 7th Doctors), both she and Chibnall eventually stated they would exit the show following the thirteenth series and three specials, which aired through 2022. Chibnall said "Jodie and I made a 'three series and out' pact with each other at the start of this once-in-a-lifetime blast. So now our shift is done, and we're handing back the Tardis keys. Jodie's magnificent, iconic Doctor has exceeded all our high expectations."
David Tennant returned to portray the Doctor, only this wasn't the 10th (it was a new Doctor with an old face). Russell T. Davies was also brought back as showrunner... he and Tennant were basically the BBC's attempt to tap into nostalgia and bring back viewers after the decline caused by Chibnall's run. Tennant portrayed the "14th Doctor" for a series of a specials made in honor of the show's 60th anniversary, ones that reunited him with Donna Noble (Catherine Tate).
Rwandan-Scottish actor Ncuti Gatwa was announced in May 2022 as taking over as the Doctor from actress Jodie Whittaker in the role (following Tennant's series of specials). The Dec 25 2023 Christmas Special marked the official start of his run as the 15th Doctor, with Ncuti being the first black actor to portray the role (and the 2nd youngest actor to portray the character after Matt Smith). The Doctor underwent the legendary "Bi-Generation", a rare transformation for Galifreyans. Meaning that his future self was brought to this point in time, and when the 14th has experienced all sorts of things (like therapy and family life with Donna Noble) and finally regenerates in Ncuti... that version will then pop up here in a timey wimey loop. Gatwa said, "This role and show means so much to so many around the world, including myself, and each one of my incredibly talented predecessors has handled that unique responsibility and privilege with the utmost care. I will endeavour my utmost to do the same."
item.dc:creator: Pasquinel
